title: On the role of crack electrolyte wetting in the degradation and performance of battery active particles
authors: S. Luza-Vega, Y. Zhao, E. Martínez-Pañeda
abstract: Cathode particle fracture is widely recognised as a major degradation mechanism in lithium-ion batteries, yet cracking also permits electrolyte wetting of newly exposed internal surfaces, modifying interfacial reaction pathways. The mechanistic role of electrolyte wetting in redistributing reactions within cracked particles remains unclear. Here, we isolate this effect through a controlled comparison between (i) a fully coupled electro-chemo-mechanical model resolving lithium concentration, electrostatic potential, and stress fields in both the active material and the electrolyte inside and outside cracks, and (ii) a single-particle chemo-mechanical model employing the conventional uniform flux assumption. The coupled model predicts strong spatial heterogeneity in interfacial reaction rates, with flux amplification approximately 8x relative to the imposed uniform flux at the crack tip. Reaction redistribution, and thus lithium flux, is governed predominantly by local solid-state lithium concentration and stress variations, while electrolyte potential gradients inside cracks remain secondary under the conditions considered. Uniform flux models can underpredict delivered capacity by 25% at 1C-rate; this discrepancy increases at higher rates. They also underestimate tensile stresses throughout the delithiation process by 10%, directly affecting crack driving conditions. These results demonstrate that neglecting crack-electrolyte coupling leads to systematic underestimation of both utilisation limits and fatigue-relevant stress histories.
summary: This paper demonstrates that electrolyte infiltration into battery particle cracks significantly alters electrochemical reaction rates and mechanical stress profiles. By using a coupled electro-chemo-mechanical model, the authors show that standard models neglecting crack-electrolyte coupling systematically underestimate both battery capacity and fatigue-inducing stresses.
main_problem: Investigating how electrolyte wetting within cracks in Ni-rich cathode particles redistributes electrochemical reactions and affects battery degradation and performance.
main_result: The study finds that electrolyte wetting causes a 8x amplification of lithium flux at crack tips and that neglecting this coupling leads to a 25-32% underestimation of delivered capacity and a 10% underestimation of tensile stresses.
method: A controlled comparison between a fully coupled electro-chemo-mechanical finite element model and a simplified single-particle model using a uniform flux assumption.
model_or_system: A cracked Ni-rich layered oxide cathode particle (radius 5 um) embedded in a composite electrode containing a conductive carbon binder and liquid electrolyte.
key_observables: Lithium concentration, electrostatic potential, stress fields, interfacial reaction rates (flux), delivered capacity, and tensile stresses.
important_parameters: 1C and 3C discharge rates, lithiation/delithiation-induced volumetric changes, and crack geometry (length 1.77 um, width 0.078 um).
main_assumptions: The electrolyte is treated as a binary liquid; cracks are assumed to be stationary; the electrolyte domain is a homogenized representation of liquid and carbon binder; no CEI formation is considered.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the problem overview and model geometries; Figure 2 shows equilibrium potential and molar volume vs stoichiometry; Figure 3 shows electrolyte potential distribution; Figure 4 shows spatial reaction rate and overpotential distribution; Figure 5 shows lithium concentration and hydrostatic stress; Figure 6 shows lithium flux evolution; Figure 8 shows stress and chemical strain ratios.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of particle cracking, presents the two modeling frameworks (Realistic vs. Idealized wetting), details the governing physics and numerical implementation, compares results regarding flux redistribution and concentration gradients, evaluates the impact on capacity and stress, and concludes with the implications for battery degradation modeling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
